coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of N,S dual heteroatom-doped fluorescent carbon dots (N,S-CDs) and their applications in gentamicin sensing and dual-switch anti-counterfeiting encryption
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Long, Xiang
Liu, Hongxiang
Hu, Jing
Wu, Shaogui
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report a facile one-step solvothermal synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped fluorescent carbon dots (N,S-CDs) using benzoic acid as the carbon source and sulfonamide as the doping agent. The synthesized N,S-CDs exhibit stable photoluminescence with an impressive fluorescence quantum yield of 16.8 %. These N,S-CDs maintain their luminescent properties under various challenging conditions, including high salt concentrations, elevated temperatures, UV radiation exposure, acidic or alkaline environments, long-term storage, and different solvents. Notably, the photoluminescence of N,S-CDs is excitation-wavelength dependent, with emission wavelengths tunable from 470 nm to 585 nm. Furthermore, the N,S-CDs display distinct fluorescent hues in aqueous and ethanolic media, presenting a novel dual-switch anti-counterfeiting ink system. Leveraging the sensitivity of N,S-CDs to gentamicin, we have developed a facile and rapid detection method for this antibiotic, which has been successfully applied to the analysis of authentic milk samples. This research contributes a novel and efficient protocol for the fabrication of high-performance carbon dots, thereby expanding their potential applications in food analysis and anti-counterfeiting technologies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Anti-counterfeiting encryption
Carbon dots
Gentamicin sensing
Heteroatom doping

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application of N,S-CDs in fluorescent inks
Visualized ratiometric fluorescence detection of gentamicin (GM)
Mechanism of gentamicin (GM) quenching of N,S-CDs fluorescence

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characterization. a) TEM image, particle size distribution (down right inset) , b) HRTEM, c) XRD spectrum, d) Infrared spectrum, e) XPS survey spectrum, f-i) High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, and S 2p.

Optical Properties of N,S-CDs. a) The excitation-emission matrix, b) Ultraviolet–visible absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra, c) CIE coordinate, d) Normalized PL spectra at different excitation wavelengths.

a) Photographs of N,S-CDs in different polarity solvents under daylight and 365 nm UV light, b) PL emission spectra of N,S-CDs dissolved in various solvents under 466 nm excitation, c) Fluorescence intensity, d) Fitting curves of integrated fluorescence intensity against absorbance in different solvents, e) Quantum yields in different solvents.
Stability of N,S-CDs. a) Salt resistance, b) Photobleaching resistance, c) Temporal stability, d) Temperature stability. e) Impact of pH. f) Solvent stability.
a) Fluorescent inks for pen, stamp, inkjet printer. b) The physical picture of dual-switch anti-counterfeiting filter paper, c) Fluorescent ink for hand-writing.

a) The selectivity of N, S-CDs to different antibiotics and amino acids. b) The anti-interference of N, S-CDs to antibiotics and amino acids, c) The change of fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs to GM concentration (0–62.5 μM), d) The linear relationship of fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs in the range of 0–62.5 μM of GM concentration.
a) UV spectra of N, S-CDs, GM and the complex of N, S-CDs with GM, b) Fluorescence lifetime spectra. c) UV absorption spectra of GM and the maximum excitation spectra of N, S-CDs.
Comparison of GM detection by different probes.
Detection of GM in milk samples.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of N,S dual heteroatom-doped fluorescent carbon dots (N,S-CDs) and their applications in gentamicin sensing and dual-switch anti-counterfeiting encryption

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu, 610066, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu, 610066, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report a facile one-step solvothermal synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped fluorescent carbon dots (N,S-CDs) using benzoic acid as the carbon source and sulfonamide as the doping agent. The synthesized N,S-CDs exhibit stable photoluminescence with an impressive fluorescence quantum yield of 16.8 %. These N,S-CDs maintain their luminescent properties under various challenging conditions, including high salt concentrations, elevated temperatures, UV radiation exposure, acidic or alkaline environments, long-term storage, and different solvents. Notably, the photoluminescence of N,S-CDs is excitation-wavelength dependent, with emission wavelengths tunable from 470 nm to 585 nm. Furthermore, the N,S-CDs display distinct fluorescent hues in aqueous and ethanolic media, presenting a novel dual-switch anti-counterfeiting ink system. Leveraging the sensitivity of N,S-CDs to gentamicin, we have developed a facile and rapid detection method for this antibiotic, which has been successfully applied to the analysis of authentic milk samples. This research contributes a novel and efficient protocol for the fabrication of high-performance carbon dots, thereby expanding their potential applications in food analysis and anti-counterfeiting technologies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) represent a novel category of nanomaterials distinguished by their superior optical characteristics, biocompatibility, and low toxicity [1–3]. In recent years, continuous research has expanded CDs' applications across diverse fields. For instance, Zhang et al. reported new advancements in light-responsive CDs for tumor therapy [4]. Long et al. demonstrated the use of CDs to enhance chloride binding energy in cement, thereby inhibiting steel corrosion [5]. Zhao et al. developed an active packaging film incorporating CDs for food preservation [6]. Jeong et al. created a CD-based fluorescent sensor for Fe3+ detection in water [7], while Amit et al. applied CDs for photocatalytic degradation of rhodamine B [8]. Besides, They are poised to revolutionize the fields of fluorescent anti-counterfeiting and encryption due to their distinctive optical signatures. CDs can be integrated into intricate fluorescent patterns or barcodes that remain imperceptible under standard lighting but become discernible upon exposure to specific light wavelengths, thereby significantly augmenting the complexity and security of anti-counterfeiting strategies [9,10]. By manipulating factors such as particle size and surface chemistry, the emission spectra of CDs can be diversified, facilitating multi-wavelength encoding [11,12]. This approach yields an extensive array of potential code combinations, enabling the creation of billions of unique identifiers and bolstering the security of anti-counterfeiting measures. CDs also present several advantages, including robust stability, cost-effectiveness, accessibility of raw materials, high biocompatibility, and non-toxicity. The application of this technology is extensive, particularly in the anti-counterfeiting of high-value items such as automotive components, electronics, pharmaceuticals, and luxury commodities. It serves to deter the proliferation of counterfeit and substandard products, safeguarding consumer interests and preserving brand integrity.

Gentamicin (GM), a broad-spectrum aminoglycoside antibiotic, targets the ribosome of pathogens to inhibit protein synthesis, thereby exerting its antimicrobial action [13,14]. GM is extensively utilized in the treatment of bacterial infections, particularly those caused by Gram-negative bacteria, and is also prevalent in the livestock industry for the prevention and treatment of animal infections [15,16]. Consequently, GM residues are occasionally detected in agricultural products. The presence of antibiotic residues in the food supply can pose health risks, prompting the Ministry of Agriculture of the People's Republic of China to establish a maximum residue limit (MRL) of 200 μg/L (0.445 μM) for GM in milk. Consequently, the surveillance of GM content in the environment and the food chain is of utmost importance. Current quantitative detection methods for GM encompass high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [17], liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [18–20], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) [21–23], and electrochemical techniques [24–26]. However, these methodologies necessitate costly equipment and are characterized by protracted analysis times and intricate procedures. There is an evident demand for a straightforward, cost-effective, and expedited GM detection method.

In this research, we introduce a straightforward one-step solvothermal synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs), employing benzoic acid and sulfanilamide as the precursors. The synthesized nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) exhibit exceptional photoluminescence stability and achieve a notable fluorescence quantum yield of 17 %. These N,S-CDs demonstrate remarkable resilience, maintaining their fluorescent properties under a wide range of challenging conditions. These include high sodium chloride concentrations, elevated temperatures, xenon lamp illumination, exposure to strong acids and alkalis, and prolonged UV light irradiation. Moreover, the N,S-CDs display a distinctive excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior. This unique characteristic allows for precise tuning of the emission wavelength across a broad spectrum, ranging from 470 nm to 585 nm, simply by adjusting the excitation wavelength. This versatility in emission control enhances the potential applications of these N,S-CDs in various fields, including sensing, imaging, and anti-counterfeiting technologies. Additionally, the N,S-CDs exhibit varying fluorescent colors in water and ethanol, facilitating the creation of a dual-switch anti-counterfeiting ink system. Moreover, capitalizing on the sensitivity of N,S-CDs to gentamicin, we have developed a straightforward and efficient method for detecting this antibiotic. This study presents a facile and effective strategy for the synthesis of high-performance CDs, thereby expanding their potential applications in the realm of food analysis and other sectors.

Benzoic acid, sulfonamide, ethanol, tetrahydrofuran, acetonitrile, acetone, N,N-dimethylformamide, and NaCl were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemicals Co., Ltd. Quinine sulfate was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. All these reagents were analytical grade. Deionized water was prepared in-house through double distillation.

Hydrothermal reaction vessels (25 mL) were obtained from Xiniu Technology Co., Ltd. An ultrasonic cleaner was purchased from Shanghai Fuyuan Instrument Co., Ltd. A hot air oven was acquired from Shanghai Lichen Technology Co., Ltd. A vacuum dryer was also obtained from Xiniu Technology Co., Ltd. UV–vis spectra were measured using a Shenyu HP756PC UV–visible spectrophotometer, and fluorescence spectra were obtained from a Lingguang F98 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The morphology and microstructure of N, S-CDs were studied by Talos F200X S transmission electron microscope (TEM). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was acquired using an IR960 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a 250Xi instrument to determine the elemental composition and chemical states of the N,S-CDs. The fluorescence lifetime of N,S-CDs was measured by TCSPC Fluorescence Lifetime Analyzer.

0.5 g (5 mmol) benzoic acid and 0.75 g (5 mmol) sulfonamide were added to 10 mL of ethanol, and the resulting mixture was ultrasonicated for 10 min to obtain a homogeneous mixture. The mixture was subsequently transferred to a 25 mL high-pressure reactor vessel and heated at 180 °C for 6 h under solvothermal conditions. After the reaction, the solution was centrifuged at 15,000 rpm for 10 min to separate insoluble substances. The supernatant was collected and dialyzed in a dialysis pocket (MWCO:0–500) for 72 h, yielding the N,S-co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) solution. The specific process is shown in Fig. 1.

The relative fluorescence quantum yield is the ratio of the number of photons emitted by the fluorescent material to the number of photons absorbed. It is a key parameter used to evaluate the luminescence efficiency of fluorescent materials. In the current work, we used a 0.1 M sulfuric acid solution of quinine sulfate as the standard (quantum yield of 0.54 [27,28]) and employed the following formula to calculate the fluorescence quantum yield of the samples:
(1)
Q Y =
F u
F r
×
A r
A u
×
η u 2
η r 2
Where, QY is the fluorescence quantum yield; F u and F r are the integrated areas of the fluorescence emission spectra of the sample and the standard, respectively; A u and A r are the absorbances of the sample and the standard, respectively; η u and η r are the refractive indices of the solvents used for the sample and the standard, respectively.

Accurately transfer 50 μL of N,S-CDs solution (0.678 mg/mL) into a 2 mL cuvette, followed by the addition of gentamicin solutions at varying concentrations and 2 mL of distilled water. After a 2-min reaction period, measure the emission spectrum using an excitation wavelength of 466 nm, recording the fluorescence intensity as F. The fluorescence intensity without gentamicin is denoted as F0. Perform six parallel measurements and calculate the average. Subsequently, derive the linear regression equation. For the milk sample preparation, take 100 mL of milk and adjust its pH to 4.47 using acetic acid. Sonicate the mixture for 20 min, then centrifuge at 4000 rpm for 10 min, retaining the supernatant for further use. In a 2 mL cuvette, combine 50 μL of N,S-CDs solution (0.678 mg/mL) with 2 mL of the clarified milk solution. Add a specific concentration and volume of gentamicin to this mixture and allow it to react for 2 min. Measure the emission spectrum at an excitation wavelength of 466 nm, recording the fluorescence intensity as F. Conduct six parallel measurements and calculate the average value. Finally, determine the concentration of gentamicin using the previously established linear regression equation.

The characterization of the N,S-CDs was conducted using a suite of analytical techniques, including transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). TEM analysis, as depicted in Fig. 2 a, disclosed that the N,S-CDs possessed an irregular shape with a mean diameter of approximately 6.8 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) (Fig. 2b) displayed lattice fringes with a 0.21 nm spacing, aligning with the (100) lattice plane of graphitic carbon, indicative of the carbon dots' graphitic nature. The XRD pattern, shown in Fig. 2c, featured a pronounced and sharp peak at approximately 25°, associated with the (002) plane of graphitic carbon, signifying the development of a highly crystalline graphitic structure during carbonization. An additional peak at around 41° corresponded to the (100) plane of graphite, further corroborating the graphitic-like structure of the N,S-CDs. FTIR spectroscopy, as illustrated in Fig. 2d, detected an array of functional groups on the surface of the N,S-CDs, with peaks observed at 3650 cm−1 for O–H/N–H stretching, 2890 cm−1 for C–H stretching, a range of 1500–1700 cm−1 for C=O/C=N stretching, 1328 cm−1 for C–N stretching, and at 1130 cm−1 and 678 cm−1 for C–O and S–C stretching, respectively. XPS analysis, presented in Fig. 2e, confirmed the elemental composition of the N,S-CDs, with carbon (C), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), and sulfur (S) present in relative atomic percentages of 76.64 %, 11.16 %, 9.92 %, and 2.28 %, respectively. High-resolution XPS spectra, depicted in Fig. 2f–i, delineated the chemical states of these elements, with C 1s exhibiting peaks for C–C/C=C, C–N/C–O/C–S, and C=O; O 1s displaying peaks for C–OH, C–O–C, and O=C; N 1s showing peaks for pyrrolic N and graphitic N; and S 2p highlighting peaks for S–C and S–H. Collectively, these findings indicate that the N,S-CDs consist of a graphitic carbon core adorned with surface functional groups that incorporate N and S heteroatoms.

The photophysical characteristics of the N,S-CDs were investigated through their excitation-emission matrix (EEM) fluorescence spectrum, UV–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum, and fluorescence emission spectrum. The EEM fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 3 a) indicated that the emission spectrum of the carbon dots spanned from 433 nm to 656 nm, peaking prominently at 520 nm. The UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 3b) displayed a pronounced absorption band at 228 nm, which is characteristic of the π-π* transition in aromatic sp2-hybridized domains [28], and a broad absorption feature at 352 nm, ascribed to the n-π* transition of the C=O groups [29]. The fluorescence emission spectrum (Fig. 3c) identified the peak excitation and emission wavelengths for the N,S-CDs as 466 nm and 520 nm, respectively. The chromaticity coordinate (CIE) chromaticity coordinates (Fig. 3c) verified the green fluorescence emission of the synthesized N,S-CDs. Remarkably, the normalized fluorescence spectra (Fig. 3d) highlighted an excitation-wavelength-dependent emission behavior, with the peak emission wavelength progressively shifting from 470 nm to 585 nm as the excitation wavelength increased from 360 nm to 550 nm. This behavior is attributed to the quantum confinement effect, wherein the size of the carbon dots influences their electronic energy levels and, by extension, their emission properties. Smaller carbon dots, with higher energy levels, emit at shorter wavelengths, whereas larger carbon dots emit at longer wavelengths. This tunable emission property, modulated by excitation wavelength, is advantageous for applications such as multicolor fluorescent labeling and imaging. By adjusting the excitation wavelength, the emission color can be altered, enhancing the informational capacity of imaging techniques.

The solvatochromic effect of N,S-CDs were assessed in a range of solvents, including water, ethanol (EtOH), acetone (ACE), dimethylformamide (DMF), acetonitrile (ACN), and tetrahydrofuran (THF), revealing solvent-dependent variations in their fluorescence behavior, as depicted in Fig. 4 . Photographs of N,S-CDs in the aforementioned solvents under ambient light and ultraviolet (UV) light (365 nm) are presented in Fig. 4a. The sequence of solvents from left to right is EtOH, DMF, ACN, water, ACE, and THF. The solutions exhibited a light brown hue under daylight, but under UV illumination, they emitted distinct fluorescence colors: blue in DMF and EtOH, blue-green in ACN, light green in THF and AC, and yellow-green in water. This solvent-induced color change is akin to "solvatochromism," a phenomenon observed with organic dyes, and is typically ascribed to the influence of solvent polarity and intramolecular interactions.

Take 2 mL of the above solvent, add 50 uL of 0.678 mg/mL N,S-CDs, and measure their emission spectra. The emission spectra of N,S-CDs in the various solvents, when excited at 466 nm, are displayed in Fig. 4b. It can be seen from Fig. 4c that the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs was most intense in DMF, followed by a descending order of ACN, EtOH, ACE, THF, and water. Despite the differences in solvents, the emission peaks of N,S-CDs, excited at 466 nm, were centered around 520 nm. The observed color variation under 365 nm UV light is primarily attributed to solvent relaxation or dipole complexation effects around the N,S-CDs, which enhances the Stokes shift to higher wavelengths.

Quantum yield measurements of N,S-CDs in the six solvents were conducted, with quinine sulfate serving as a reference standard. By plotting the integrated fluorescence intensity against absorbance at varying concentrations (Fig. 4d), the relative quantum yields of the N,S-CDs in the different solvents were calculated. As illustrated in Fig. 4e, the quantum yield of N,S-CDs varied significantly across the solvents, generally aligning with the observed fluorescence intensity. The highest quantum yield was recorded in DMF (16.8 %), while the lowest was observed in water (7.7 %), reflecting the substantial impact of the solvent environment on the optical properties of the N,S-CDs.

The stability of the fluorescence properties of carbon dots is essential for their practical utility. Herein, the stability of N,S-CDs' fluorescence properties was comprehensively evaluated under diverse environmental conditions to assess their practical utility. Initially, salt resistance was determined by immersing N,S-CDs in NaCl solutions ranging from 0 to 1000 mM. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the fluorescence intensity remained largely invariant even at the highest NaCl concentration, demonstrating excellent resistance to saline conditions. Photobleaching resistance was examined by exposing the solution to UV light (365 nm, 48 W) for 2 h, with samples taken every 20 min. Fig. 5b illustrates only a slight reduction in fluorescence intensity, indicating commendable photostability. Storage stability was evaluated by monitoring the fluorescence spectra of N,S-CDs solution stored at low temperature in darkness over a week, with Fig. 5c showing nearly constant fluorescence intensity, suggesting prolonged preservation without significant deterioration. Thermal stability was explored by recording fluorescence spectra at temperatures ranging from 10 °C to 60 °C, with Fig. 5d demonstrating only a minimal decrease in intensity with rising temperature. The effect of pH on fluorescence intensity was also tested, with Fig. 5e showing minimal changes in the pH range of 2–13, with quenching observed only in strongly acidic conditions (pH = 1). This is because under strong acid conditions, functional groups such as O–H, N–H, and –COOH on the surface of N,S-CDs interact with each other, causing N,S-CDs aggregation and fluorescence quenching. Finally, the fluorescence stability of carbon dots in different solvents was measured. The fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs in different solvents before and after 2 h was measured, and the results are shown in Fig. 5fIt can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs did not change significantly after being placed in different solvents for 2 h, indicating that N,S-CDs have good stability in different solvents. These comprehensive results demonstrate the exceptional stability of N,S-CDs across a spectrum of environmental conditions, including high salt concentrations, UV radiation exposure, long-term storage, elevated temperatures, varying pH levels, and different solvents. This robust fluorescence performance underscores the promising practical applications of N,S-CDs, ensuring their reliability and longevity in diverse environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N,S-CDs in fluorescent inks
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the stable and excellent fluorescence properties of N,S-CDs, which exhibit different fluorescence in ethanol and water, their potential use in anti-counterfeiting and encryption as a dual-switch ink was explored. As shown in Fig. 6 a, it can be used for writing, printing, stamping, and writing purposes. N,S-CDs solution was applied to qualitative acid-free filter paper cut into a ‘flower’ shape, as shown in Fig. 6b. Under 365 nm UV light, the filter paper emitted intense blue fluorescence. Upon the addition of distilled water, a faint blue fluorescence appeared on the filter paper. Subsequently, the addition of ethanol restored the blue fluorescence, enabling information encryption. Similarly, because N,S-CDs display different fluorescence in ethanol and water, their ethanol and water solutions can be used as fluorescent inks for writing. As depicted in Fig. 6c, the written content is nearly invisible under daylight but becomes brightly fluorescent under 365 nm UV light. Once the UV lamp is turned off, the fluorescence completely vanishes, achieving information encryption under daylight and decryption under UV light. It is evident that N,S-CDs hold significant potential in the field of fluorescent anti-counterfeiting printing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualized ratiometric fluorescence detection of gentamicin (GM)
ce:section.ce:para: The specificity of N,S-CDs for various antibiotics and amino acids was initially assessed. As illustrated in Fig. 7 a, the N,S-CDs demonstrated a marked selectivity for gentamicin (GM) over a range of other antibiotics and amino acids tested, including amoxicillin (AMC), tetracycline (TC), penicillin (PNC), kanamycin (KAN), chloramphenicol (CPL), erythromycin (EM), oxytetracycline (OTC), aureomycin (CTC), streptomycin (SM), ceftazidime (CAZ), and several amino acids such as l-phenylalanine (L-Phe), l-glutamic acid (L-Glu), l-lysine (L-Lys), l-histidine (L-His), l-threonine (L-Thr), ascorbic acid (ASA), l-hydroxyproline (L-Hyp), and glycine (Gly). The influence of these additional substances on the detection of GM was also investigated, with results (Fig. 7b) indicating that the presence of other analytes had a minimal impact on the fluorescence response of N,S-CDs to GM. Subsequently, N,S-CDs were employed as a fluorescent sensing platform for the quantification of GM. The fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs (excited at λex = 466 nm) exhibited a gradual decrease with increasing concentrations of GM, as shown in Fig. 7c. A linear correlation between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F 0/F) and the concentration of GM was established in the concentration range of 0–62.5 μM, as depicted in Fig. 7d. The linear equation was determined to be F 0/F = 0.01282C(GM) + 1.00373 (R 2 = 0.9964), where C(GM) is the GM concentration, and F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of GM, respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 0.42 μM. In comparison to other detection methods reported in the literature, as compiled in Table 1 , the N,S-CDs-based assay for GM offers a lower detection limit, highlighting its superior sensitivity and efficiency. This method's enhanced capability for GM detection underscores its potential as a valuable tool in the monitoring of antibiotic residues, particularly in food safety and environmental monitoring applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of gentamicin (GM) quenching of N,S-CDs fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: The primary mechanisms by which fluorescent molecules are quenched by known quenchers encompass static quenching (SQ), dynamic quenching (DQ), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), and the inner filter effect (IFE). As depicted in Fig. 8 a, the introduction of GM into the N,S-CDs solution led to a notable shift in the absorption spectrum, implying the formation of a non-emissive ground-state complex between the N,S-CDs and GM. The fluorescence lifetime of N,S-CDs before and after the addition of GM was measured, and the results are shown in Fig. 8b. It can be seen that the fluorescence lifetime of N,S-CDs did not change significantly, indicating that the fluorescence quenching of N,S-CDs is static quenching. In addition, as shown in Fig. 8c, comparing the absorption spectrum of GM with the maximum excitation spectrum of N,S-CDs, one can see that there is no overlapping region between the two. This finding suggests that the quenching of N,S-CDs fluorescence by GM is not a result of the inner filter effect. Based on these observations, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of N,S-CDs by GM is ascribed to static quenching. To showcase the practical utility of the N,S-CDs-based fluorescence sensing platform with real-world samples, cow milk was selected as a representative sample matrix. The milk samples underwent pre-treatment following the protocol outlined in Section 2.4. Recovery experiments were carried out by fortifying the milk samples with various concentrations of GM, thereby assessing the method's accuracy in real sample analysis. The results, as presented in Table 2 , indicated that the recoveries of GM in milk samples spanned from 93.93 % to 106.54 %, with relative standard deviations (RSD) kept below 8 %. These findings affirm the method's high accuracy and precision for the detection of GM in actual milk samples, underscoring its significant potential for real-world applications. The successful application of the N,S-CDs-based fluorescence sensing platform in the detection of GM in cow milk demonstrates its efficacy beyond laboratory settings. The method's robustness, coupled with its simplicity and rapidity, makes it a promising tool for quality control and safety monitoring in the dairy industry. Furthermore, the ability to accurately quantify GM in milk samples without extensive sample preparation or the need for sophisticated instrumentation enhances the accessibility and practicality of this approach. The potential for extending this method to other types of food products and environmental samples remains an exciting avenue for future research, with the overarching goal of ensuring food safety and public health.

In this work, we present a facile one-step solvothermal method for the synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) using benzoic acid and sulfonamide as precursors. The as-prepared N,S-CDs exhibit excellent optical properties, including stable photoluminescence, a high quantum yield of 16.8 %, and tunable excitation-dependent emission spanning from 470 nm to 585 nm. The structural characterization confirms the graphitic-like core structure of the N,S-CDs with surface functional groups containing N and S heteroatoms. The N,S-CDs demonstrate remarkable stability under various environmental conditions, including high salt concentrations, UV irradiation, long-term storage, strong acid and alkali, elevated temperatures, and different solvents, indicating their great potential for practical applications. The N,S-CDs also display distinct fluorescence colors in different solvents, which has been leveraged to develop a dual-switch anti-counterfeiting ink system for security applications. Furthermore, the N,S-CDs show excellent selectivity and sensitivity towards the detection of the antibiotic gentamicin (GM), with a low detection limit of 0.42 μM. The quenching mechanism is attributed to static quenching between the N,S-CDs and GM. The successful application of the N,S-CDs-based sensing platform for the detection of GM in real milk samples further validates the practicality and reliability of this method. This multifaceted study not only advances the understanding of heteroatom doping effects on carbon dots but also expands their practical applications, paving the way for future innovations in material science.

Xiang Long: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Hongxiang Liu: Investigation, Validation. Jing Hu: Investigation. Shaogui Wu: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thank Prof. Dongmao Zhang, Ph.D Joanna Xiuzhu Xu, Xue Leng et al. in Department of Chemistry, Mississippi State University for helpful advices.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent carbon dots are the new quantum dots: an overview of their potential in emerging technologies and nanosafety
J Mater Sci

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Alas MO, Alkas FB, Aktas Sukuroglu A, Genc Alturk R, Battal D. Fluorescent carbon dots are the new quantum dots: an overview of their potential in emerging technologies and nanosafety. Journal of Materials Science. 2020;55:15074-15105.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review of fluorescent carbon dots, their synthesis, physical and chemical characteristics, and applications
Nanomaterials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jorns M, Pappas D. A review of fluorescent carbon dots, their synthesis, physical and chemical characteristics, and applications. Nanomaterials. 2021;11(6):1448.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: a new type of carbon-based nanomaterial with wide applications
ACS Cent Sci

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Liu J, Li R, Yang B. Carbon dots: a new type of carbon-based nanomaterial with wide applications. ACS Central Science. 2020;6(12):2179-2195.

Responsive Materials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang H, Liu Y, Qu S. Recent advances in photo-responsive carbon dots for tumor therapy. Responsive Materials. 2024;2(2):e20240012.
